title: Samguk Yusa: The Myth of Tangun
description: Korea's foundation myth: the son of heaven descends to earth, a bear becomes a woman, and their child Tangun founds the nation.
introduction: The Tangun myth, preserved in the 13th-century Samguk Yusa, is the foundation narrative of the Korean people. Hwanung, son of the Lord of Heaven, descends to Mount Taebaek to establish a holy city. A bear endures 100 days of fasting in a cave and is transformed into a woman; she and Hwanung produce Tangun, who founds Gojoseon — the first Korean kingdom — in 2333 BCE. The myth remains central to Korean national identity.

translation.original_text: 古記云。昔有桓因。庶子桓雄。數意天下。
translation.text: The old records say: in ancient times, Hwanin had a son, Hwanung, who desired to descend and govern the world of men.

translation.original_text: 時有一熊一虎。同穴而居。常祈桓雄。願化為人。
translation.text: At that time a bear and a tiger lived together in a cave. They prayed constantly to Hwanung, wishing to be transformed into human beings.
